branch_key: any-any-any
stage: any

living_situation: any
try_this_today: "Pick one person to tell, and one task to defer for two weeks. The rest can wait."
---

## What this is

Someone you care about has been diagnosed with dementia. Maybe it is a parent, a spouse, a sibling, an in-law, a friend you promised to look after. Maybe you are the obvious person for this, or maybe you are the one who happened to be in the room. However you got here, the appointment is behind you, and you are now holding a word that rearranges things. This module covers the first two weeks — what is normal to feel, what can wait, and the small number of things worth doing now.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
Most people describe the hours after a dementia diagnosis as a strange split: part of them is relieved that there is finally a name for what has been happening — the repeated questions, the missed appointments, the moments that did not add up — and part of them is grieving a future that just quietly closed. The person they love is still the same person they were this morning. Nothing visible has changed. But the internal list of assumptions about the future — about how things would go, about who would need what from whom — has rearranged itself without asking permission. Holding both the relief and the grief at the same time is not a sign of confusion. It is an accurate reading of the situation.

How the diagnosis was made may look different from what you expected. In 2025–2026, many clinicians use a blood test — measuring a protein called phosphorylated tau — as part of the workup [1][2][3]. The Alzheimer's Association published its first clinical guideline on these tests in July 2025, reporting that they agree with the traditional PET brain scan 91.7% of the time when positive and 97.3% when negative [1]. Your doctor may have used one of these newer blood tests, a PET scan, a spinal tap, or a combination. The method does not change what to do this week — and a separate module covers the diagnostic pathway in depth.

## Why it matters now

The first two weeks are when caregivers are most likely to make decisions they later regret — selling a house, quitting a job, telling everyone at once — or to freeze and do nothing, including things that genuinely matter [5][7]. The 2024 Lancet Commission found that timely support immediately following diagnosis reduces long-term caregiver distress [7]. This is not a crisis. It is a transition, and transitions have a shape.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
It is common in the first few days to feel a pull toward action — researching care facilities at 2 a.m., reorganizing the kitchen, sending a long email to every family member explaining what happened. The impulse makes sense: if the future is uncertain, doing something feels like control. But many caregivers find, looking back, that the decisions they made in the first seventy-two hours were the ones they most wanted to undo. Not because the decisions were wrong, but because they were made on a nervous system that had not yet steadied. The things that helped most turned out to be small and boring: writing down what the doctor said, scheduling the follow-up, eating a meal, going to bed.

Most of what feels urgent right now does not have to be solved this week. See the **Things That Can Wait** checklist for the full list, but the short version: long-term financial planning, choosing a care community, telling extended family in a particular order, changing the will, making big changes to living arrangements, arguments about who should do what. None of these get worse if you take ten days to breathe first.

## What to do this week

Pick one or two of these. Not all of them. The **First Two Weeks Action Items** checklist has the details.

**Write down what the clinician said** while you still remember: the type of dementia named (or "not yet specified"), any medications started, what tests are pending, and when the follow-up is [5]. If you did not get clear answers, send a message to the clinic. You are allowed to ask. You are allowed to ask twice.

**Tell one person.** Not the whole family, not a group text. One person you trust who can sit with the news without trying to fix it or making it about themselves [6]. If there is no one safe in your life right now, the Alzheimer's Association 24/7 Helpline (1-800-272-3900) is staffed by licensed clinicians [5].

<!-- provenance: composite -->
Many caregivers describe the moment of telling someone as harder than the appointment itself. There is the question of who — not the person who will panic, not the one who will minimize, but the one who can just hear it. You may not plan what to say. You may call someone, start with something ordinary, and then it comes out. The conversation will not fix anything. But afterward, the weight will be in two bodies instead of one, and that turns out to matter more than any advice.

**Ask the clinic if they participate in the GUIDE program.** The CMS GUIDE Model went live at 390 organizations in July 2025 [4]. If the clinic participates, your family member may be eligible for care navigation, caregiver education, and respite support — reimbursed up to roughly $2,500 per year [4]. One phone call can tell you.

**Locate key documents.** Find the most recent driver's license, insurance card, and any existing power of attorney or healthcare proxy. You do not need to act on them yet. You just need to know where they are. If they do not exist, or if you do not know where they are, that is an early priority — but it is a week-three task, not a tonight task [5].

**Know that treatment options may come up.** Your clinician may mention disease-modifying therapies that did not exist a few years ago [7]. That is a conversation between you, the person diagnosed, and the clinical team — a month-one topic, not a week-one decision.

**Take care of the basics.** Eat something. Sleep if you can. Cancel one thing on your calendar this week that is not essential. The body keeps the score, and you are going to need it [6].

## When to call sooner

Some things in the first two weeks warrant a call back to the clinician — do not wait for the next appointment.

- **Sudden increased confusion over hours or days.** A noticeable jump in confusion can signal delirium from an infection (urinary tract infections are common), a medication interaction, or another treatable cause [7]. Delirium is not the dementia getting worse overnight — it is a medical emergency layered on top, and it is treatable when caught early.
- **New medication side effects.** If a medication was started and the person develops nausea, dizziness, falls, or new agitation — call [5][6].
- **Safety concerns today.** If driving feels unsafe right now, call the clinic; they have a protocol for this [5]. If the person is wandering, leaving the stove on, or getting lost in familiar places — call.
- **Sharp mood change.** If the person diagnosed becomes markedly depressed, withdrawn, or expresses hopelessness after the diagnosis — call. Depression after a dementia diagnosis is common and treatable [5][6].
- **Talk of self-harm or harm to others.** Call the clinic, or contact emergency services. That is not an overreaction [5][6].

If **you** are the one not okay — if you are having thoughts of hurting yourself, or you cannot keep yourself or the person you care for safe — contact emergency services or the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. That is the system working as intended.

## How to care for yourself in this season

This is grief, even though no one has died. The person you care about is still here, still themselves in many ways. And a particular version of the future — yours and theirs — is not. Both of those things are real at the same time, and that doubleness is what makes the early weeks so disorienting [7]. You do not have to name it as grief yet if that word feels too big. You can call it tired. You can call it weird. You can call it nothing at all.

<!-- provenance: composite -->
What is rarely said out loud is that the grief of these first two weeks can feel illegitimate. No one has died. The person is right there. And yet something enormous has shifted. Caregivers often describe being surprised by what actually helps in this window: not researching everything, not joining a support group on day three, but something smaller — canceling one plan, sitting down for a full meal, walking to the end of the block and back. The smallness of it feels like it should not be enough. But in a week when the nervous system is running on adrenaline and bad sleep, lowering the bar is not giving up. It is the first real thing most people do for themselves.

The Lancet Commission found that even minimal early intervention — a single counseling session, a peer-support contact, a structured information packet — reduced longer-term caregiver distress [7]. You do not need to sign up for a support group tonight. But the Alzheimer's Association [5] and the National Institute on Aging [6] both maintain free caregiver resource centers designed for exactly this moment. They are there when you are ready, not before.

Whatever your relationship to this person — however you got to this page — you are here, and that matters. You do not have to have it figured out. You do not have to be the right person, or the best person, or the person who knows what to do. You are the person who showed up. That is enough for this week.

Do not make any decisions you would not make on a regular Tuesday. The ground will steady, slowly, and when it does you will think more clearly than you can right now. The next module is there when you are ready, and not before.
